---
title: "Tagma"
subtitle: "A Content‑Addressable Structural Primitive Defined by the Hangul Syllabic Space"
date: 2026-07
date-format: "MMMM, YYYY"
version-prefix: tagmawp-0.1
version-mark: true
bibliography: ./_include/reference.bib
csl: ./_include/ieee.csl
date-modified: last-modified
generate-title-meta: false
metadata-files:
    - ./_include/author.founder.yml
    - ./_include/format.pdf.yml
    - ./_include/format.html.yml
colorlinks: true
abstract: |
  Project Tagma defines a structural identity primitive for computing derived from the coordinate system embedded in Hangul — the only script whose Unicode encoding follows a closed-form composition formula. The standard allocates a contiguous block of 11,172 complete syllables, each decomposable into three independent axes that map to a 16-bit hardware coordinate space. In this space, every valid value serves as its own content-addressable identifier, replacing hash-based identity generation with a combinational decoder. We describe the coordinate space, the decoder specification, a RISC-V coprocessor implementation path, and a future direction extending the same principle into the memory subsystem.
format:
  html:
    number-sections: true
    code-font: "Source Code Pro"
    appendix-style: plain
  pdf:
    geometry:
        - margin=26mm
    keep-tex: true
    number-sections: true
    execute:
        echo: false
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: darkgray
    citecolor: gray
    urlcolor: gray
    code-block-bg: false
    code-block-border-left: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{xcolor}
        \usepackage{microtype}
        \setkeys{Gin}{width=\linewidth,height=\textheight,keepaspectratio}
        \addtokomafont{disposition}{\rmfamily}
        \input{./_files/wp_metadata.tex}
        \DeclareTOCStyleEntry[beforeskip=6pt]{default}{section}
        \DeclareTOCStyleEntry[beforeskip=0pt]{default}{subsection}
        \makeatletter
        \def\@maketitle{
          \begin{center}
            {\LARGE \bfseries \@title \par}
            \vspace{1em}
            {\large \@subtitle \par}
            \vspace{4em}
                {\large \@author \thanks{\authorrole; Corresponding author: \texttt{\href{mailto:\authoremail}{\authoremail}}} \par}
                \vspace{0.3em}
                {\normalsize \affiliationname \par}
                \vspace{0.3em}
                {\normalsize \texttt{\href{\affiliationurl}{\affiliationdomain}} \par}
            \vspace{2em}
                {\normalsize \@date \par}
          \end{center}
          \vspace{4em}
        }
        \makeatother
---

```{python}
#| include: false
#| context: local
%run ./_include/_graphviz.py
```

## Introduction

Every content-addressable system in computing today generates identifiers through a hash function. This indirection layer imposes a predictable cost: gate area for the hash logic, cycle latency for computation, and storage overhead for the resulting identifier. Hash functions are a general solution but not the only one. When the identifier space has known structural properties, those properties can replace the hash function entirely.

Tagma is a specification for a silicon block that uses such a structure --- the closed-form composition formula of the Unicode Hangul syllable block [@unicode-hangul-block; @unicode-chapter3] --- to derive identity directly from value composition. It occupies a specific layer: the boundary between binary representation and content-addressable identity. A coordinate can be displayed as a Hangul syllable for debugging, decomposed into its three axis values for assembly, or processed as a raw 16-bit value by the hardware. The Projector layer that performs these translations is a software interface and is not part of the decoder specification.

Tagma is not a language, an encoding standard, or an instruction set architecture. It is specified as a gate-level combinatorial decoder that would attach to a RISC-V pipeline [@structural-computing]. The name derives from the Greek "syntagma," meaning a well-ordered arrangement of constituent elements.

## The Hangul Coordinate Space

Hangul was created in 1443 under a documented philosophical and structural design [@humminjeongeum]. Consonants are derived from articulator shapes; vowels from heaven-earth-human triadic symbolism [@kang-hangul-phonetics]. The Unicode standard encodes this design as a closed-form composition formula [@iso10646-hangul]:

$$C(i,m,f) = \text{U+AC00} + 588i + 28m + f, \quad 0 \leq i < 19,\; 0 \leq m < 21,\; 0 \leq f < 28$$

Only Hangul satisfies all four conditions required for the Tagma primitive: a fixed closed-form composition formula, complete fixed-width 16-bit representation, a structural invalidity margin, and an open international standard.

```{python}
#| include: false
import numpy as np

# Valid syllable lattice dimensions
n_init, n_med, n_fin = 19, 21, 28
stride = 2
init_vals = np.arange(0, n_init, stride)
med_vals  = np.arange(0, n_med,  stride)
fin_vals  = np.arange(0, n_fin,  stride)

I, M, F = np.meshgrid(init_vals, med_vals, fin_vals, indexing='ij')
I_f, M_f, F_f = [a.flatten() for a in (I, M, F)]

# Box edge helper
def box_edges(x0, y0, z0, x1, y1, z1):
    edges = []
    def add(xa, ya, za, xb, yb, zb):
        edges.append(((xa, ya, za), (xb, yb, zb)))
    for (dx, dy, dz) in [(x1,y0,z0),(x0,y1,z0),(x0,y0,z1)]:
        add(x0,y0,z0,dx,dy,dz)
    add(x1,y1,z0, x1,y0,z0); add(x1,y1,z0, x0,y1,z0); add(x1,y1,z0, x1,y1,z1)
    add(x0,y1,z1, x1,y1,z1); add(x0,y1,z1, x0,y0,z1); add(x0,y1,z1, x0,y1,z0)
    add(x1,y0,z1, x1,y1,z1); add(x1,y0,z1, x1,y0,z0); add(x1,y0,z1, x0,y0,z1)
    return edges

full_edges  = box_edges(-1, -1, -1, 32, 32, 32)
valid_edges = box_edges(-0.5, -0.5, -0.5, 18.5, 20.5, 27.5)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-3d-decode
#| fig-cap: "Three-dimensional bitcell organization of the Tagma coordinate space. The 19 x 21 x 28 valid syllable lattice (shaded volume) occupies a subvolume of the full 5-bit-per-axis addressable space (dashed wireframe). Each dot corresponds to one valid Hangul syllable coordinate addressable by three independent decoders."
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d import Poly3DCollection

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(11, 9))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')

# draw valid region as a semi-transparent filled box
# face vertices for the 19x21x28 box
box = np.array([[-0.5, -0.5, -0.5],
                [18.5, -0.5, -0.5],
                [18.5, 20.5, -0.5],
                [-0.5, 20.5, -0.5],
                [-0.5, -0.5, 27.5],
                [18.5, -0.5, 27.5],
                [18.5, 20.5, 27.5],
                [-0.5, 20.5, 27.5]])

# Define the 6 faces of the box (quadrilaterals)
faces = [[0,1,2,3], [4,5,6,7], [0,1,5,4], [2,3,7,6], [0,3,7,4], [1,2,6,5]]

valid_volume = Poly3DCollection([box[f] for f in faces], alpha=0.12, facecolor='#2c7bb6', edgecolor='none')
ax.add_collection3d(valid_volume)

# Lattice points: bigger markers, full density
ax.scatter(I_f, M_f, F_f,
           c=F_f, cmap='viridis', s=18, alpha=0.85, linewidth=0.2,
           edgecolor='white', vmin=0, vmax=27, depthshade=True)

# Full addressable space (dashed wireframe)
for (xa, ya, za), (xb, yb, zb) in full_edges:
    ax.plot([xa, xb], [ya, yb], [za, zb], color='#888888', linestyle='--', linewidth=0.5)
ax.plot([], [], [], color='#888888', linestyle='--', linewidth=0.5, label='Full 32x32x32 addressable space')

# Valid region edge (solid wireframe) — draw on top of the volume
for (xa, ya, za), (xb, yb, zb) in valid_edges:
    ax.plot([xa, xb], [ya, yb], [za, zb], color='#1a3a5c', linestyle='-', linewidth=0.8)
ax.plot([], [], [], color='#1a3a5c', linestyle='-', linewidth=0.8, label='Valid 19x21x28 syllable region')

# Annotate the Unicode block range
ax.text(18.5, 20.5, 30,
        'U+AC00 .. U+D7AF\n11,172 valid coordinates',
        fontsize=8, ha='center', va='bottom',
        bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.3', facecolor='white', edgecolor='#1a3a5c', alpha=0.9))

ax.set_xlabel('Initial (choseong)', fontsize=11)
ax.set_ylabel('Medial (jungseong)', fontsize=11)
ax.set_zlabel('Final (jongseong)', fontsize=11)
ax.set_xlim(-3, 35)
ax.set_ylim(-3, 35)
ax.set_zlim(-3, 35)

ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(plt.MaxNLocator(5))
ax.yaxis.set_major_locator(plt.MaxNLocator(5))
ax.zaxis.set_major_locator(plt.MaxNLocator(5))

# Clean panes
ax.xaxis.pane.fill = False
ax.yaxis.pane.fill = False
ax.zaxis.pane.fill = False
ax.xaxis.pane.set_edgecolor('none')
ax.yaxis.pane.set_edgecolor('none')
ax.zaxis.pane.set_edgecolor('none')
ax.xaxis.line.set_color('black')
ax.yaxis.line.set_color('black')
ax.zaxis.line.set_color('black')
ax.tick_params(axis='x', colors='black', labelsize=9)
ax.tick_params(axis='y', colors='black', labelsize=9)
ax.tick_params(axis='z', colors='black', labelsize=9)

# Light grid on axes planes for depth perception
for coord in range(0, 33, 8):
    ax.plot([coord, coord], [0, 31], [0, 0], color='#dddddd', linewidth=0.3, zorder=-1)
    ax.plot([0, 31], [coord, coord], [0, 0], color='#dddddd', linewidth=0.3, zorder=-1)

ax.view_init(elev=24, azim=-68)
ax.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize='small', facecolor='white', edgecolor='#cccccc', framealpha=0.95)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

This 19 x 21 x 28 lattice occupies 11,172 of the 65,536 states representable in 16 bits; the remaining 54,364 are structurally invalid.

```{python}
#| label: fig-hangul-axes
#| fig-cap: "The three structural axes of the Hangul syllable coordinate space."
dot("""
digraph HangulAxes {
    rankdir=LR;
    node [shape=box];
    syllable [label="Hangul syllable\n16-bit coordinate\nU+AC00..U+D7AF"];
    initial [label="Initial\n19 values\n5 bits"];
    medial [label="Medial\n21 values\n5 bits"];
    final [label="Final\n28 values\n5 bits"];
    syllable -> initial [label="  divide by 588"];
    syllable -> medial [label="  divide by 28 (remainder)"];
    syllable -> final [label="  mod 28"];
}
""")
```

| Field | Bits | Values | Range |
|-------|------|--------|-------|
| Initial (choseong) | 5 | 19 | 0-18 |
| Medial (jungseong) | 5 | 21 | 0-20 |
| Final (jongseong) | 5 | 28 | 0-27 |
| Reserved | 1 | -- | -- |

## Philosophical Grounding

In current computing, data is identified by hash --- a derived value that depends on the data but is not the data itself. The identifier is separate from the structure it identifies. This indirection has costs: generation time, collision probability, lookup infrastructure.

Tagma proceeds from a different principle: the coordinate is the structure; the structure is the identifier. A syllable coordinate is not a label assigned to a piece of data. It is a decomposition of the data into three axes whose combined values uniquely determine the coordinate, and the coordinate uniquely determines the combined values. There is no gap between what something is and what it is called.

This principle is possible because Hangul's composition formula produces a one-to-one mapping between every valid syllable and its 16-bit coordinate. No other Unicode script provides such a structure. Every other encoding uses atomic assignments inherited from ASCII [@kim-hangul-unicode]. Tagma builds on this six-century-old foundation, translating a philosophical design into a hardware primitive. The project is open-source and welcomes contributions from the community whose linguistic heritage makes this innovation possible.

## The Encoding Problem

Variable-length encoding has architectural consequences that are independent of any particular implementation. Memory alignment is not guaranteed. Instruction decoding must detect boundary positions. Prefetch and pipeline efficiency are affected. These are structural properties of variable-length design, not engineering limitations.

```{python}
#| label: fig-encoding
#| fig-cap: "Encoding hierarchy from variable-length to fixed-length structural encoding."
dot("""
digraph EncodingHierarchy {
    rankdir=TB;
    node [shape=box];
    binary [label="Binary / Transistor"];
    ascii [label="ASCII (1963)\n7-bit, variable"];
    unicode [label="Unicode (ISO 10646)\nmultiple encoding forms"];
    utf8 [label="UTF-8\n1-4 byte variable"];
    utf16 [label="UTF-16\n2-byte + surrogate"];
    tagma [label="Tagma\n16-bit fixed\nclosed-form formula\nstructural validity"];
    binary -> ascii;
    binary -> unicode;
    unicode -> utf8;
    unicode -> utf16;
    unicode -> tagma [label="  Hangul block only"];
}
""")
```

| Encoding | Storage unit | Addressable symbols | Wasted bits/unit | Fixed alignment | Structural HW check |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|----------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| ASCII | 1 byte (8 bits) | 128 of 256 | 1 bit (12.5%) | yes | no |
| Latin-1 | 1 byte | 191 of 256 | variable | yes | no |
| UTF-8 (English) | 1-4 bytes | unlimited | none for ASCII | no | no |
| UTF-16 (BMP) | 2 bytes | 63,488 of 65,536 | 2,048 reserved | no | no |
| UTF-32 | 4 bytes | unlimited | 75-88% for common use | yes | no |
| **Tagma** | **2 bytes** | **11,172 of 65,536** | **none** | **yes** | **yes (54,364 invalid)** |

The structural constraint in Tagma arises from the fixed formula by which Hangul syllables are composed. Of the 65,536 states representable in 16 bits, only 11,172 satisfy the composition rules. A hardware decoder can distinguish valid from invalid states using combinational logic derived from the Unicode composition formula [@unicode-hangul-block].

## The Identity Problem

Every system that stores data by content rather than by location must generate identifiers. Current approaches and their hardware cost:

| Method | Identifier size | Generation cost | Collision | Lookup structure |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|-----------|------------------|
| Pointer | 32-64 bits | zero | none | direct (location-based) |
| Hash (SHA-256) | 256 bits | ~10K gates, 64-75 cycles | probabilistic | hash table + resolution |
| UUID | 128 bits | entropy-dependent | probabilistic | hash table |
| CAM | per-bit comparison | 9-16 transistors/bit | none | associative [@paget-cam-survey] |
| **Tagma** | **16 bits** | **~300 gates, 1 cycle** | **none (formulaic)** | **direct (coordinate = address)** |

Hash-based identity requires a hardware SHA-256 engine, a hash table with collision resolution, and additional storage for 32-byte identifiers. Tagma replaces all of this with a combinational decoder and a 16-bit register. The trade-off is that Tagma identifiers are limited to 11,172 distinct values.

Tagma replaces the identifier generation pipeline (hash function, collision resolution, lookup table) with a direct mapping: the coordinate is the identifier.

| Property | SHA-256 based | Tagma | Factor |
|----------|-------------|-------|--------|
| Identifier size | 256 bits | 16 bits | 16x smaller |
| Generation latency | 64-75 cycles | 1 cycle (combinational) | 64-75x lower |
| Collision | probabilistic | deterministic, zero | logic eliminated |
| Hardware for generation | ~10,000 gates | ~300 gates | ~30x fewer gates |
| Lookup structure | hash table + collision list | direct address | structure eliminated |
| Maximum identifiers | unlimited | 11,172 | constrained |

Tagma addresses the class of content-addressable storage with up to approximately 11,000 entries. Systems in this range gain the efficiency of direct-mapped content addressability without CAM cell overhead [@cam-cell-design] or hash logic.

## The Decoder

The decoder extracts three 5-bit fields from a 16-bit input in three combinational stages:

```{python}
#| label: fig-decoder
#| fig-cap: "Three-stage decoder flow: range check, field extraction, validation."
dot("""
digraph DecoderFlow {
    rankdir=LR;
    node [shape=box];
    input [label="16-bit input"];
    range [label="Range\nU+AC00..U+D7AF"];
    extract [label="Field extraction\noffset / 588 / 28"];
    validate [label="Validation\ninitial<19 medial<21 final<28"];
    output [label="valid + 3 fields"];
    invalid [label="invalid flag"];
    input -> range;
    range -> extract [label="in range"];
    range -> invalid [label="out of range"];
    extract -> validate;
    validate -> output [label="pass"];
    validate -> invalid [label="fail"];
}
""")
```

**Stage 1: Range check.** Two comparators verify the input is within [U+AC00, U+D7AF]. An out-of-range value produces an immediate invalid flag.

**Stage 2: Field extraction.** For in-range values:

| Step | Operation | Output | Range |
|------|-----------|--------|-------|
| 1 | `input - 0xAC00` | offset | 0-11,171 |
| 2 | `offset \div 588` | initial | 0-18 |
| 3 | `offset \bmod 588` | remainder | 0-587 |
| 4 | `remainder \div 28` | medial | 0-20 |
| 5 | `remainder \bmod 28` | final | 0-27 |

**Stage 3: Validation.** Three comparators check: initial < 19, medial < 21, final < 28. Any failure sets the invalid flag.

Tagma coordinates support proximity-based search without dedicated search hardware. Given two coordinates, the field-wise Hamming distance is computed by three banks of parallel XOR gates. This enables associative lookup without hash tables, B-trees, or CAM cells.

| Component | Gates |
|-----------|-------|
| Range check | ~50 |
| Offset subtraction | ~80 |
| Division by 588 (multiply-shift) | ~80 |
| Division by 28 (multiply-shift) | ~60 |
| Validation comparators x3 | ~30 |
| **Total** | **~300** |

Approximately 300 gate equivalents in 28nm. The decoder is smaller than a single 32-bit multiplier.

## Coprocessor Implementation

Tagma would attach to a RISC-V pipeline through the XIF coprocessor interface defined by the OpenHW Group [@openhw-xif; @cva6-xif]. This is a standard interface that allows custom instructions without modifying the core pipeline.

```{python}
#| label: fig-stack
#| fig-cap: "Tagma position in the system stack."
dot("""
digraph TagmaStack {
    rankdir=LR;
    node [shape=box];
    app [label="Application logic"];
    riscv [label="RISC-V pipeline\n(unmodified)"];
    interface [label="XIF coprocessor interface", shape=box];
    tagma [label="Tagma decoder\n~300 gates, 1 cycle"];
    mem [label="Standard memory hierarchy\n(unmodified)"];
    app -> riscv;
    riscv -> interface [style=dashed];
    interface -> tagma;
    tagma -> mem;
}
""")
```

Three custom instructions are defined:

**tagma_check rd, rs1.** Decodes the lower 16 bits of rs1. Returns a 16-bit value: valid flag (1 bit, 1 = valid), reserved (1 bit, reads as zero), initial (5 bits), medial (5 bits), final (5 bits). Invalid inputs produce a zero valid flag and undefined field values. One cycle, combinational.

**tagma_dist rd, rs1, rs2.** Computes field-wise Hamming distance between the two 16-bit coordinates. Returns three 5-bit distances (initial, medial, final). One cycle, combinational.

**tagma_mux rd, rs1, rs2, imm5.** imm5 selects the field (0=initial, 1=medial, 2=final; values 3-31 are reserved). If the selected fields of rs1 and rs2 are equal, rd = rs1; else rd = rs2. The immediate operand is encoded in the instruction word according to the XIF specification for 5-bit immediates.

### Verification

The coordinate space is exhaustively enumerable: 65,536 values, of which 11,172 are valid. A verification harness generates all 65,536 inputs, applies the decoder specification, and records the results. The enumeration approach follows the same pattern used in RISC-V instruction space verification [@riscv-formal]. The bijection is verified by enumeration: no collisions, no unassigned values within the block.

### Architectural Mapping

Tagma maps to SSCCS structural primitives as defined in the Schema--Segment Composition Computing System [@lee-schema-segment-2026; @structural-computing]. A coordinate in this space is a Segment: a 16-bit value whose identity is its structure. The composition rules of initial, medial, and final correspond to the Scheme that defines which coordinates are valid. Validity predicates (range checks) implement the Field by defining admissible coordinate ranges. Enumerating coordinates under constraints implements Observation: a hardware-level selection of valid states.

## Comparison with Existing Paradigms

**Content-Addressable Memory** provides content-based lookup in one cycle but at 9-16 transistors per bit versus 6 for SRAM [@paget-cam-survey]. At the Tagma maximum of 11,172 entries, Tagma with direct mapping uses standard SRAM cells at approximately 2.5x less transistor count than CAM, at the cost of 54,364 unused states. Tagma is more area-efficient when the stored set is sparse; CAM is more efficient when dense.

**Hash-based lookup.** Tagma replaces the entire hash pipeline with a combinational decoder. The trade-off is identifier count versus gate, cycle, and storage cost.

**Linear memory.** Pointer-based addressing has zero generation cost and no collision, but provides no content-addressability. Tagma would provide content-addressability at the hardware level, with the address derived from structure rather than location.

**Fixed-length RISC instructions.** RISC-V solved the variable-length instruction decode problem with 32-bit fixed-length encoding [@riscv-unpriv]. Tagma applies the same principle to data identifiers: fixed-length, self-decodable, structurally verifiable.

## Memory Hierarchy Penetration

The coprocessor path (above) would attach Tagma to a linear memory system. The coordinate is decoded and the result is used as an address in conventional memory.

### Memory Controller Mapping

The Tagma coordinate space can be mapped to a physical address region configured to bypass virtual-to-physical translation, for example through RISC-V PMP or non-standard memory region attributes. The controller then receives each coordinate directly as a physical address and maps `initial-medial-final` to `row-column` within the existing memory array. Existing RISC-V platforms support custom address mapping logic at the memory controller or system level.

### Three-Dimensional Decode

The bitcell array can be organized as a 19 x 21 x 28 grid (11,172 cells) rather than a linear array. Alternatively, a memory controller can map the 11,172 valid coordinates to a dense linear array through a simple packed-index transformation. The three coordinate fields each have their own decoder:

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-decode
#| fig-cap: "Three parallel decode paths from the 16-bit coordinate fields to the bitcell array wordlines."
dot("""
digraph ThreeDecode {
    rankdir=LR;
    node [shape=box];

    input [label="16-bit coordinate\\ninitial(5) medial(5) final(5)"];

    init_dec [label="Initial decoder\\n5-to-19"];
    med_dec [label="Medial decoder\\n5-to-21"];
    fin_dec [label="Final decoder\\n5-to-28"];

    init_wl [label="Primary wordline\\n19 lines"];
    med_wl [label="Secondary wordline\\n21 lines"];
    fin_sel [label="Column select\\n28 lines"];

    bitcell [label="Bitcell array\\n19 x 21 x 28"];

    input -> init_dec [label="  initial[4:0]"];
    input -> med_dec [label="  medial[4:0]"];
    input -> fin_dec [label="  final[4:0]"];

    init_dec -> init_wl;
    med_dec -> med_wl;
    fin_dec -> fin_sel;

    init_wl -> bitcell;
    med_wl -> bitcell;
    fin_sel -> bitcell;
}
""")
```

| Parameter | Linear (SRAM) | Tagma 3D (SRAM) |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Decoder type | 1 decoder (N-to-2^N) | 3 decoders (5-to-19, 5-to-21, 5-to-28) |
| Worst-case decoder delay | O(2^N) | O(32) max |
| Address mapping | row + column | initial + medial + final |
| Address translation | required | none |
| Structural validity | external (ECC) | built-in |

The bitcell array remains standard 6T SRAM. The change is in the peripheral circuitry: three independent decoders replace the single row-column decoder pair, and the sense amplifier layout must accommodate three-axis access. This increases peripheral complexity but leaves the bitcell array unchanged [@openram].

### Current Status

The decoder specification and coordinate space definition are complete. Part I (XIF coprocessor) is the current implementation path. The decoder logic, coordinate space specification, and verification methodology follow the same pattern as the existing verification pipeline. Part II (memory controller mapping and 3D decoder layout) is specified but not yet implemented. The two paths share the identical coordinate specification and field decomposition; they differ only in the layer at which decode occurs.

## Application Domains

The deterministic single-cycle decode makes Tagma suitable for real-time and safety-critical systems. The three-decoder topology of the future path reduces worst-case decode latency compared to a single linear decoder. Structural error trapping is relevant for high-radiation environments where a baseline validity check supplements ECC. Proximity-based search supports pattern matching and nearest-neighbor lookup without specialized hardware.

## Boundaries

Tagma is a 16-bit boundary, not a 16-bit limitation. It addresses a specific design range: systems requiring content-addressable identity for a working set of up to 11,172 entries. Part I is designed to be compatible with existing RISC-V pipelines and verification flows. Part II is a future direction with no current implementation timeline. The structural validity check provided by the decoder does not eliminate the need for full error-correcting codes: single-bit errors that map one valid coordinate to another are not detected.

## Vision

Tagma defines a new type of silicon primitive: a combinational decoder that derives identity from structure at approximately 300 gates and one cycle. At this gate cost, content-addressable operation becomes viable where hash-based approaches are too expensive in power, area, or latency. The structural validity check embedded in every decode provides inherent error detection, relevant for radiation-tolerant computing in space environments. The coordinate space that enables this is Hangul — an open international standard and a public good. Tagma will be released as open-source silicon, inviting the next conversation: what else becomes possible when identity costs less than a single multiply.

## References {.unnumbered .unlisted}

::: {#refs}
:::

{{< include ./_include/_license.md >}}
